title: How to Tell the Inner Voice from Anxiety
subtitle: A practical guide to distinguishing genuine intuition from anxious noise
excerpt: A reading about the practical difference between intuition and anxiety. Covers what each one sounds like, how they feel in the body, the timing test, and what to do when they are hard to separate.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Draws on the psychological and somatic literature on interoception, decision-making, and anxiety.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you cannot tell whether what you are feeling is a genuine signal or anxious noise, or when anxiety has been loud enough for long enough to obscure your own instincts.

content.text: The inner voice and anxiety can feel remarkably similar from the inside. Both are wordless feelings that arrive without being asked for. Both produce a sense of certainty. Both can feel urgent. The difference between them is real and learnable, but it takes attention to distinguish them.
The problem of distinguishing them

For people who have lived with anxiety for a long time, the anxious voice has often been present for so long that it has become the background. It is hard to identify the signal when it has always been indistinguishable from the noise. Learning to tell them apart is less about getting a single decision right and more about developing a practice of noticing.

The other difficulty is that following the inner voice can feel uncomfortable too, while anxiety sometimes presents as certainty. Neither feeling alone is a reliable guide. You need more than one data point.
What anxiety sounds like

Anxiety tends to be loud, repetitive, and urgent. It says the same thing over and over in slightly different forms. It comes with a demand: do something, decide something, fix something now. It generates worst-case scenarios and then asks you to pre-solve them. It tends to get louder when reassurance is not provided.

Anxiety is also future-oriented. It is almost always about what might happen rather than what is happening. It creates urgency around problems that do not exist yet and may never exist. The urgency itself is the problem, not the situation being pointed at.
In the body, anxiety tends to produce constriction: the chest tightens, the breath gets shallower, the muscles around the shoulders and neck activate. The feeling is of something closing down.
What the inner voice sounds like

Genuine intuition tends to be quieter and more consistent. It does not repeat itself in increasingly urgent ways. It does not need to convince you. It says the thing once and waits. If the message is from the inner knowing, it tends to still be there when you come back to it later.

Intuition also tends to be present-oriented. It is usually about what is happening now, or about a direction rather than a specific catastrophic outcome. It does not generate worst-case scenarios. It generates a sense of direction.

In the body, intuition tends to produce a different quality of feeling from anxiety. Some people experience it as a settling or a slight opening. Others experience it as a clear, still quality of knowing that is different from the activated feeling of fear. This varies by person, which is why noticing your own particular signals matters more than following a general rule.
The timing test

One of the most reliable tests for distinguishing the two is timing. When you receive a feeling that might be intuition, wait. Come back to it in an hour, or the next morning. Ask: is the message still there? Is it the same message? Anxiety tends to escalate or change when not acted upon. Genuine inner knowing tends to remain stable.

This test does not work for every situation. Some decisions have genuine time pressure. But for most decisions that feel urgent, the urgency itself is worth examining. Real urgency rarely requires a decision in the next thirty seconds. Anxious urgency often feels like it does.
Building the practice

Telling inner voice from anxiety is a practice, not a fixed skill. It builds through small experiments: noticing the quality of a feeling, coming back to it later, checking whether it was anxiety or knowing, and updating how you read your own signals.

The first step is simply to introduce the question: Is this anxiety, or is this knowing? You do not need to be able to answer it reliably yet. Asking it is the beginning of developing the ability to answer it. Over time, the difference becomes more legible.
Where this practice comes from
Original to homemade.education. It draws on research into how the body signals knowing versus fear.
